mn51:1.2: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Campā, on the bank of the Gaggarā lotus pond, together with a large *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghena*].
mn51:1.3: Then Pessa, the elephant-rider’s son, and Kandaraka the wanderer went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to him, Pessa, the elephant-rider’s son, paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side.
mn51:1.4: But Kandaraka the wanderer exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*. When they had concluded their courteous and memorable conversation, he stood to one side.
mn51:1.5: Standing to one side, Kandaraka the wanderer, after looking over the silent, silent *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghaṁ*], said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn51:10.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the person who is both a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction, and a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others?
mn51:10.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a certain person is either a king, a consecrated noble with anointed head, or a wealthy brahmin.
mn51:10.3: He has a new sacrificial hall built to the east of the city. Then, having shaved off hair and beard, put on a rough hide, anointed his body with ghee and oil, and scratching his back with a deer horn, he enters the new sacrificial hall together with the chief queen and the brahmin chaplain.
mn51:10.4: There he makes his bed on the bare ground, freshly smeared with green cow dung.
mn51:10.5: From one cow with a calf of the same appearance, whatever milk there is in one teat, by that the king lives; whatever milk there is in the second teat, by that the chief queen lives; whatever milk there is in the third teat, by that the brahmin chaplain lives; whatever milk there is in the fourth teat, with that he makes an offering into the fire; and the calf lives on what remains.
mn51:10.7: ‘So many bulls are to be slaughtered for the sacrifice; so many bull-calves are to be slaughtered for the sacrifice; so many heifer-calves are to be slaughtered for the sacrifice; so many goats are to be slaughtered for the sacrifice; so many rams are to be slaughtered for the sacrifice; so many horses are to be slaughtered for the sacrifice; so many trees are to be cut for the sacrificial posts; so much darbha grass is to be mown for the sacrificial grass.’
mn51:10.8: And those slaves, messengers, and workers he has — they too, threatened with punishment, threatened with fear, with tearful faces and weeping, perform the tasks.
mn51:10.9: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the person who is both a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction, and a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others.
mn51:11.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the person who is neither a self-tormentor, nor devoted to the practice of self-affliction, nor a tormentor of others, nor devoted to the practice of afflicting others — not tormenting himself, not tormenting others, dwelling in this very life hungerless, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahma in himself?”
mn51:12.1: Here, *bhikkhus*, a Tathāgata arises in the world, an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, one who has gone well, knower of the world, unsurpassed guide of persons to be trained, teacher of devatās and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.
mn51:12.2: He makes known this world with its devatās, with its Māras, with its Brahmās, this generation with its ascetics and brahmins, with its devatās and humans, having directly known and realized it for himself.
mn51:12.3: He teaches the *dhamma*, good in the beginning, good in the middle, good in the end, with meaning and phrasing; he makes known the holy life that is entirely complete and pure.
mn51:13.1: A householder, or a householder’s son, or someone born in some other family hears that *dhamma*.
mn51:13.2: Having heard that *dhamma*, he gains confidence in the Tathāgata.
mn51:13.3: Endowed with that gaining of confidence, he reflects thus:
mn51:13.4: ‘The household life is crowded, a path of dust; going forth is wide open.
mn51:13.5: It is not easy, while living in a house, to live the holy life utterly complete, utterly pure, polished like a conch shell.
mn51:13.6: What if I were to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the household life into homelessness?’
mn51:13.7: After some time, having abandoned a small mass of wealth or a great mass of wealth, having abandoned a small circle of relatives or a great circle of relatives, he shaves off hair and beard, puts on ochre robes, and goes forth from the household life into homelessness.
mn51:14.1: Having thus gone forth, he undertakes the training and livelihood of *bhikkhus*. Abandoning the killing of living beings, he abstains from killing living beings. With rod laid aside, with weapon laid aside, conscientious, compassionate, he dwells caring for the welfare of all living beings.
mn51:14.10: He abstains from dancing, singing, music, and shows.
mn51:14.11: He abstains from garlands, fragrances, ointments, adornment, and embellishment.
mn51:14.12: He abstains from high and luxurious beds.
mn51:14.13: He abstains from accepting gold and silver.
mn51:14.14: He abstains from accepting raw grain.
mn51:14.15: He abstains from accepting raw meat.
mn51:14.16: He abstains from accepting women and girls.
mn51:14.17: He abstains from accepting female and male slaves.
mn51:14.18: He abstains from accepting goats and sheep.
mn51:14.19: He abstains from accepting chickens and pigs.
mn51:14.2: Abandoning taking what is not given, he abstains from taking what is not given. He takes only what is given, expecting only what is given, and dwells pure in himself, not stealing.
mn51:14.20: He abstains from accepting elephants, cattle, horses, and mares.
mn51:14.21: He abstains from accepting fields and land.
mn51:14.22: He abstains from running errands and carrying messages.
mn51:14.23: He abstains from buying and selling.
mn51:14.24: He abstains from false weights, false metals, and false measures.
mn51:14.25: He abstains from bribery, deception, fraud, and trickery.
mn51:14.26: He abstains from wounding, killing, imprisoning, robbery, plunder, and violence.
mn51:14.3: Abandoning the unholy life, he lives the holy life, living apart, abstaining from the sexual act, the village practice.
mn51:14.4: Abandoning false speech, he abstains from false speech. He speaks truth, is trustworthy, reliable, steadfast, not a deceiver of the world.
mn51:14.5: Abandoning divisive speech, he abstains from divisive speech. What he has heard here he does not tell there to break up those people, and what he has heard there he does not tell here to break up these people. Thus he is a reconciler of those divided, a promoter of those united, one who delights in harmony, rejoices in harmony, enjoys harmony, and speaks words that make for harmony.
mn51:14.6: Abandoning harsh speech, he abstains from harsh speech. He speaks such words as are gentle, pleasing to the ear, lovable, going to the heart, courteous, desired by many, agreeable to many.
mn51:14.7: Abandoning idle chatter, he abstains from idle chatter. He speaks at the right time, speaks what is factual, what is beneficial, speaks *dhamma*, speaks *vinaya*; at the right time he speaks words worth treasuring, measured, circumscribed, connected with benefit.
mn51:14.8: He abstains from damaging seeds and plant life.
mn51:14.9: He eats once a day, abstaining from food at night, refraining from eating at improper times.
mn51:15.1: He is content with robes to protect the body and with almsfood to sustain the belly. Wherever he goes, he goes taking only these along.
mn51:15.2: Just as a bird, wherever it flies, flies with only its wings as its burden,
mn51:15.3: so too a *bhikkhu* is content with robes to protect the body and with almsfood to sustain the belly. Wherever he goes, he goes taking only these along.
mn51:15.4: Endowed with this noble aggregate of ethical conduct, he experiences within himself a blameless *sukha*.
mn51:16.1: Seeing a form with the eye, he is not one who grasps at *nimittas* [*nimittaggāhī*] or at secondary features.
mn51:16.2: Since, if he were to dwell with the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*] unguarded, bad unskillful *dhammas* of desire and distress might invade him, he practices for its restraint, guards the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], and undertakes restraint of the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriye saṁvaraṁ*].
mn51:16.3: Hearing a sound with the ear …
mn51:16.4: Smelling an odor with the nose …
mn51:16.5: Tasting a flavor with the tongue …
mn51:16.6: Touching a tangible with the body …
mn51:16.7: Cognizing a *dhamma* with the mind, he is not one who grasps at *nimittas* [*nimittaggāhī*] or at secondary features.
mn51:16.8: Since, if he were to dwell with the mind *indriya* [*manindriyaṁ*] unguarded, bad unskillful *dhammas* of desire and distress might invade him, he practices for its restraint, guards the mind *indriya* [*manindriyaṁ*], and undertakes restraint of the mind *indriya* [*manindriye saṁvaraṁ*].
mn51:16.9: Endowed with this noble guarding of the *indriyas* [*indriyasaṁvarena*], he experiences within himself an unblemished *sukha*.
mn51:17.1: He acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking away; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in wearing his outer robe, bowl, and robes; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in defecating and urinating; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent.
mn51:18.1: Endowed with this noble aggregate of ethical conduct, endowed with this noble contentment, endowed with this noble guarding of the *indriyas* [*indriyasaṁvarena*], and endowed with this noble *sati* and full awareness [*satisampajaññena*],
mn51:18.2: he resorts to a secluded resting place: a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a hillside cave, a charnel ground, a jungle grove, an open space, or a heap of straw.
mn51:19.1: After the meal, on returning from almsround, he sits down, folding his legs crosswise, setting his body upright, and establishing *sati* [*satiṁ upaṭṭhapetvā*] in front of him.
mn51:19.2: Abandoning desire for the world, he dwells with a mind [*cetasā*] free from desire, and he purifies the mind [*cittaṁ*] of desire.
mn51:19.3: Abandoning ill will and hostility, he dwells with a mind [*citta*] free from ill will, compassionate for the welfare of all living beings, and he purifies the mind [*cittaṁ*] of ill will and hostility.
mn51:19.4: Abandoning dullness and drowsiness, he dwells free from dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, with *sati* [*sato*], fully aware [*sampajāno*], and he purifies the mind [*cittaṁ*] of dullness and drowsiness.
mn51:19.5: Abandoning restlessness and remorse, he dwells unagitated, with his mind [*citta*] inwardly stilled, and he purifies the mind [*cittaṁ*] of restlessness and remorse.
mn51:19.6: Abandoning doubt, he dwells having gone beyond doubt, not uncertain about skillful *dhammas*, and he purifies the mind [*cittaṁ*] of doubt.
mn51:2.1: “It is astonishing, Master Gotama, it is amazing, Master Gotama.
mn51:2.2: How well Master Gotama has guided the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅgho*].
mn51:2.3: Those *Bhagavās* too who were *arahants*, perfectly awakened ones, in the past — they too guided the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghaṁ*] in just this highest way—
mn51:2.4: just as Master Gotama now has guided the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅgho*].
mn51:2.5: And those *Bhagavās* who will be *arahants*, perfectly awakened ones, in the future — they too will guide the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghaṁ*] in just this highest way—
mn51:2.6: just as Master Gotama now has guided the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅgho*].”
mn51:20.1: Having abandoned these five hindrances, corruptions of the mind [*cetaso*] that weaken discernment [*paññā*],
mn51:20.2: quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn51:21.1: With the stilling of thought [*vitakkavicārānaṁ*] and examination [*vitakkavicārānaṁ*], he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, which has internal clarity and unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of the mind [*cetaso*], without thought [*avitakkaṁ*], without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijaṁ*].
mn51:22.1: And with the fading away of *pīti*, he dwells in equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati*, fully aware [*sampajāno*], and experiences *sukha* with the body. He enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones say: ‘One who has equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* dwells in *sukha*.’
mn51:23.1: With the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier ending of joy and distress, he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant, purified by *sati* and equanimity [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn51:24.1: When his mind [*citta*] is thus *samādhi*-composed [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of corruption, pliable, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he inclines the mind [*cittaṁ*] to the knowledge of recollecting past lives.
mn51:24.2: He recollects his manifold past lives, that is: one birth, two births, three births, four births, five births, ten births, twenty births, thirty births, forty births, fifty births, a hundred births, a thousand births, a hundred thousand births, many eons of world-contraction, many eons of world-expansion, many eons of world-contraction and expansion: ‘There I was so named, of such a clan, of such an appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a life-span. Passing away from there, I arose elsewhere; and there too I was so named, of such a clan, of such an appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a life-span. Passing away from there, I arose here.’ Thus he recollects his manifold past lives in their modes and details.
mn51:25.1: When his mind [*citta*] is thus *samādhi*-composed [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of corruption, pliable, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he inclines the mind [*cittaṁ*] to the knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings.
mn51:25.2: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, he sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in fortunate destinations and in unfortunate destinations, and he understands beings according to their *kamma*: ‘Indeed, these worthy beings, who were endowed with bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct, who reviled the noble ones, were of wrong view, and undertook action based on wrong view — with the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a state of loss, a bad destination, ruin, hell. But these worthy beings, who were endowed with bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct, who did not revile the noble ones, were of right view, and undertook action based on right view — with the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a fortunate destination, a heavenly world.’ Thus with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, he sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in fortunate destinations and in unfortunate destinations, and he understands beings according to their *kamma*.
mn51:26.1: When his mind [*citta*] is thus *samādhi*-composed [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of corruption, pliable, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he inclines the mind [*cittaṁ*] to the knowledge of the destruction of the *āsavas*.
mn51:26.2: He understands as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*.’ He understands as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*.’ He understands as it truly is: ‘This is the ending of *dukkha*.’ He understands as it truly is: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the ending of *dukkha*.’
mn51:27.1: He understands as it truly is: ‘These are the *āsavas*.’ He understands as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of the *āsavas*.’ He understands as it truly is: ‘This is the ending of the *āsavas*.’ He understands as it truly is: ‘This is the way of practice leading to the ending of the *āsavas*.’
mn51:27.2: For one knowing thus and seeing thus, the mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated from the *āsava* of sensuality, the mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated from the *āsava* of *bhava*, the mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjā*].
mn51:27.3: When it is liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn51:27.4: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn51:28.1: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the person who is neither a self-tormentor, nor devoted to the practice of self-affliction, nor a tormentor of others, nor devoted to the practice of afflicting others.
mn51:28.2: Not tormenting himself, not tormenting others, in this very life he dwells hungerless, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahma in himself.”
mn51:28.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn51:3.1: “That is so, Kandaraka, that is so, Kandaraka.
mn51:3.10: Here, Kandaraka, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world;
mn51:3.11: he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world;
mn51:3.12: he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*], ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world;
mn51:3.13: he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world.”
mn51:3.2: Those *Bhagavās* too who were *arahants*, perfectly awakened ones, in the past — they too guided the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghaṁ*] in just this highest way—
mn51:3.3: just as I now have guided the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅgho*].
mn51:3.4: And those *Bhagavās* who will be *arahants*, perfectly awakened ones, in the future — they too will guide the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghaṁ*] in just this highest way—
mn51:3.5: just as I now have guided the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅgho*].
mn51:3.6: For, Kandaraka, there are *bhikkhus* in this *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] who are *arahants*, with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavā*], who have lived the holy life, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, reached their own goal, with the fetters of *bhava* entirely destroyed, completely liberated through final knowledge.
mn51:3.7: And, Kandaraka, there are *bhikkhus* in this *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghe*] who are trainees, of constant ethical conduct, of constant way of conduct, prudent, living prudently.
mn51:3.8: They dwell with their mind well established [*suppatiṭṭhitacittā*] in the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.
mn51:4.1: When this was said, Pessa, the elephant-rider’s son, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn51:4.10: How it is that the *Bhagavā*, while there is such a tangle of humans, such a thicket of humans, such human deviousness, knows what is beneficial and what is harmful for beings.
mn51:4.13: For I, *bhante*, am capable of guiding an elephant to be trained.
mn51:4.14: In whatever interval it will make the journey to and from Campā, it will reveal all its deviousnesses, all its frauds, all its crookednesses, all its twists.
mn51:4.15: But our slaves, messengers, and workers behave one way with body, another way with speech, and their mind [*citta*] is yet another way.
mn51:4.16: It is astonishing, *bhante*, it is amazing, *bhante*.
mn51:4.17: How it is that the *Bhagavā*, while there is such a tangle of humans, such a thicket of humans, such human deviousness, knows what is beneficial and what is harmful for beings.
mn51:4.3: How well these four *satipaṭṭhānas* have been set forth by the *Bhagavā* for the purification of beings, for the overcoming of sorrow and lamentation, for the ending of pain and distress, for the attainment of the way, for the realization of *nibbāna*.
mn51:4.4: For we too, *bhante*, as householders dressed in white, from time to time dwell with our mind well established [*suppatiṭṭhitacittā*] in these four *satipaṭṭhānas*.
mn51:4.5: Here we, *bhante*, dwell contemplating body in body, ardent, fully aware [*sampajānā*], with *sati* [*satimanto*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world;
mn51:4.6: we dwell contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajānā*], with *sati* [*satimanto*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world;
mn51:4.7: we dwell contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*], ardent, fully aware [*sampajānā*], with *sati* [*satimanto*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world;
mn51:4.8: we dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajānā*], with *sati* [*satimanto*], having removed desire and distress in relation to the world.
mn51:4.9: It is astonishing, *bhante*, it is amazing, *bhante*.
mn51:5.1: “That is so, Pessa, that is so, Pessa.
mn51:5.10: Not tormenting himself, not tormenting others, in this very life he dwells hungerless, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahma in himself.
mn51:5.11: Which of these four persons pleases your mind?”
mn51:5.12: “This person, *bhante*, who is a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction — this person does not please my mind.
mn51:5.13: And this person, *bhante*, who is a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others — this person too does not please my mind.
mn51:5.14: And this person, *bhante*, who is both a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction, and a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others — this person too does not please my mind.
mn51:5.15: But this person, *bhante*, who is neither a self-tormentor, nor devoted to the practice of self-affliction, nor a tormentor of others, nor devoted to the practice of afflicting others — not tormenting himself, not tormenting others, in this very life he dwells hungerless, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahma in himself—
mn51:5.16: this is the person who pleases my mind.”
mn51:5.2: For humans, Pessa, are a tangle;
mn51:5.4: These four kinds of persons are found existing in the world, Pessa.
mn51:5.6: Here, Pessa, one person is a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction.
mn51:5.7: Here, Pessa, one person is a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others.
mn51:5.8: Here, Pessa, one person is both a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction, and a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others.
mn51:5.9: Here, Pessa, one person is neither a self-tormentor, nor devoted to the practice of self-affliction, nor a tormentor of others, nor devoted to the practice of afflicting others.
mn51:6.1: “But why, Pessa, do these three persons not please your mind?”
mn51:6.10: for this reason that person pleases my mind.
mn51:6.12: We have many duties, much to do.”
mn51:6.13: “Now is the time, Pessa, to do as you think fit.”
mn51:6.14: Then Pessa, the elephant-rider’s son, having delighted in and approved of what the *Bhagavā* said, rose from his seat, paid homage to the *Bhagavā*, and keeping him to his right, departed.
mn51:6.2: “This person, *bhante*, who is a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction, torments and afflicts himself, though he wants *sukha* and recoils from *dukkha*—
mn51:6.3: for this reason that person does not please my mind.
mn51:6.4: And this person, *bhante*, who is a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others, torments and afflicts another, though that other wants *sukha* and recoils from *dukkha*—
mn51:6.5: for this reason that person does not please my mind.
mn51:6.6: And this person, *bhante*, who is both a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction, and a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others, torments and afflicts both himself and another, though both want *sukha* and recoil from *dukkha*—
mn51:6.7: for this reason that person does not please my mind.
mn51:6.8: But this person, *bhante*, who is neither a self-tormentor, nor devoted to the practice of self-affliction, nor a tormentor of others, nor devoted to the practice of afflicting others — not tormenting himself, not tormenting others, in this very life he dwells hungerless, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahma in himself;
mn51:6.9: he torments and afflicts neither himself nor another, though both want *sukha* and recoil from *dukkha*—
mn51:7.1: Then, not long after Pessa, the elephant-rider’s son, had departed, the *Bhagavā* addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn51:7.3: Pessa, *bhikkhus*, the elephant-rider’s son, has great discernment [*paññā*].
mn51:7.4: If Pessa, *bhikkhus*, the elephant-rider’s son, had sat for a little while, until I had analyzed these four persons in detail, he would have been connected with great benefit.
mn51:7.5: But even by this much, *bhikkhus*, Pessa, the elephant-rider’s son, is connected with great benefit.”
mn51:7.6: “Now is the time, *Bhagavā*, now is the time, Fortunate One,
mn51:7.7: for the *Bhagavā* to analyze these four persons in detail. Having heard it from the *Bhagavā*, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn51:7.8: “Then listen, *bhikkhus*, and attend closely. I will speak.”
mn51:8.1: “And what, *bhikkhus*, is the person who is a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction? Here, *bhikkhus*, a certain person is naked, rejecting conventions, licking his hands, not coming when asked, not staying when asked; he does not accept food brought to him, nor food specially made for him, nor an invitation.
mn51:8.10: Or he is one who squats, devoted to striving in the squatting posture.
mn51:8.11: Or he is one who uses a bed of thorns, making his bed on a thorny couch.
mn51:8.12: Or he dwells devoted to the practice of descending into water three times daily, including the evening.
mn51:8.13: Thus he dwells devoted to the practice of heating and afflicting the body in these many various ways.
mn51:8.14: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the person who is a self-tormentor, devoted to the practice of self-affliction.
mn51:8.2: He does not accept from the mouth of a pot, or from the mouth of a bowl, or across a threshold, or across a stick, or across a pestle, or from two people eating together, or from a pregnant woman, or from a woman giving suck, or from a woman together with a man, or from where food is announced, or from where a dog is waiting, or from where flies swarm about. He does not eat fish or meat, nor does he drink liquor, wine, or fermented brew.
mn51:8.3: He goes to one house only, taking one morsel; or he goes to two houses, taking two morsels … or he goes to seven houses, taking seven morsels.
mn51:8.4: He lives on one offering; or he lives on two offerings … or he lives on seven offerings.
mn51:8.5: He takes food once a day; or once every two days … or once every seven days. Thus he dwells devoted to the practice of taking food at intervals of half a month.
mn51:8.6: He is an eater of greens, or an eater of millet, or an eater of wild rice, or an eater of leather parings, or an eater of moss, or an eater of rice bran, or an eater of scum, or an eater of oil-cake, or an eater of grass, or an eater of cow dung. He lives on forest roots and fruits, feeding on fallen fruit.
mn51:8.7: He wears hemp cloth, or mixed hemp cloth, or shrouds, or rag-robes, or bark fiber garments, or antelope skin, or a strip of antelope skin, or kusa grass garments, or bark garments, or wood-shaving garments, or a hair blanket, or a horsehair blanket, or an owl-wing blanket.
mn51:8.8: He is one who tears out hair and beard, devoted to the practice of tearing out hair and beard.
mn51:8.9: Or he is one who stands continually, rejecting seats.
mn51:9.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the person who is a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others?
mn51:9.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a certain person is a sheep butcher, a pig butcher, a bird catcher, a hunter, a trapper, a fisherman, a thief, an executioner of thieves, a cow slaughterer, a jailer, or else one engaged in any other cruel occupation.
mn51:9.3: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the person who is a tormentor of others, devoted to the practice of afflicting others.
